title_of_original: (2006/638/EC) Commision Decision on the aid scheme implemented by Italy for certain undertakings for collective investment in transferable securities specialised in shares of small- and medium-capitalisation companies listed on regulated markets
case_description: [1] On 15 February 2011, the French Government adopted Decree-Law No. 153 (DL 153/2011) [2]as part of its strategy to stimulate economic growth and improve public finances. [3] The decree entered into force on 3 September 2011, following its publication in the French Official Journal, [4] although it was not notified in advance to the European Commission. [5] A central element of DL 153/2011, Article 6, introduced a preferential tax regime for certain undertakings for collective investment in transferable securities (UCITS) that focus on acquiring shares of small and medium-sized enterprises listed on regulated markets within the European Union. [6] These entities, referred to as specialised investment structures, target companies with a market capitalisation below EUR 600 million, [7] based on the average share price on the final trading day of each quarter. [8] Under the general French tax framework for collective investments, [9] the applicable tax is withheld annually by the investment undertaking. [10] The taxable base corresponds to the annual increase in the daily recorded value of the fund's assets, representing its net operating result. [11] This mechanism is intended to tax potential capital gains at the fund level, [12] so that investors are not subject to additional taxation when income is distributed. [13] Within this context, the decree introduced a reduced substitute tax rate of 5 percent on the capital income of qualifying specialised investment structures, [14] in contrast to the standard corporation tax rate of 12.5 percent. [15] To benefit from this reduced rate, a fund must either invest at least two-thirds of its total assets in shares of small or mid-cap companies for a minimum of one-sixth of the trading days in the calendar year, [16] or must include in its governing documents a primary investment objective that focuses on such companies. [17] The French authorities published an estimate of the fiscal expenditure associated with the decree, [18] indicating a loss of tax revenue for the French Treasury. [19] The introduction of this favourable regime increased the attractiveness of specialised investment funds, leading to higher demand for their shares. [20] As a result, the funds themselves or their managing entities were able to increase management and entry fees. [21]In addition, the tax incentive encouraged investment in small and medium-sized companies, [22] which contributed to greater liquidity in that segment of the market.
gold_parsing: channeled_secondary_effects('tax reduction', 'Specialised Investment Vehicles', 'increased demand for shares'). % [19]
channeled_secondary_effects('tax reduction', 'Small and Midsized Companies', 'increased investment and liquidity in market segment'). %[21],[22]
group_of_undertakings('Specialised Investment Vehicles', 'increased demand for shares'). % [5]
group_of_undertakings('Small and Midsized Companies', 'increased investment and liquidity in market segment'). % [6]
